exercises.title: Trace test lifecycle execution order
hints: BeforeEach runs before EACH test
AfterEach runs after EACH test
Pattern: B→test→A repeats for each test

exercises.title: Write your first @Test
exercises.scenario: assertEquals is the most common assertion.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to write a test for String.length().

blanks.hint: JUnit 5 annotation that marks a test method
Most common assertion: checks exact equality

hints: @Test marks the method as a test case
assertEquals(expected, actual) — expected first!

exercises.title: Test a simple Calculator class
exercises.instructions:
Write `CalculatorTest` with three tests for:
- `add(3, 4)` → 7
- `subtract(10, 3)` → 7
- `multiply(6, 7)` → 42

Each test must pass.
hints: Use assertEquals(expected, actual) for each
Name pattern: method_scenario_expected

exercises.title: assertThrows — test exception throwing
exercises.scenario: Test that divide by zero throws ArithmeticException.
exercises.instructions:
Write a test that verifies `Calculator.divide(10, 0)` throws `ArithmeticException` with message 'Cannot divide by zero'.

Add a `divide(int a, int b)` method to Calculator first.
hints: assertThrows(ExType.class, () -> methodCall())
assertThrows returns the exception — check .getMessage()

exercises.title: @BeforeEach for shared setup
exercises.instructions:
Write `BankAccountTest` with @BeforeEach creating a fresh account (balance ₹1000), and three tests:
- Initial balance is 1000
- Deposit 500 → balance is 1500
- Withdraw 200 → balance is 800
hints: @BeforeEach runs before EACH test — fresh account every time
Without BeforeEach, tests interfere with each other

exercises.title: assertAll — multiple assertions in one test
exercises.scenario: assertAll runs all assertions even when one fails.
exercises.instructions: Write a test for a `User` class. Use assertAll to verify all fields: name='Aisha', age=28, city='Hyderabad'. Also write a second test that verifies a failed assertAll reports all failures.
hints: assertAll('heading', () -> assert1, () -> assert2, () -> assert3)
All three run even if one fails

exercises.title: Test a ProductValidator
exercises.scenario: Multiple validation rules each get their own test.
exercises.instructions:
Write tests for `ProductValidator.validate(Product p)`:
- Valid product passes without exception
- Blank name throws IllegalArgumentException
- Price <= 0 throws IllegalArgumentException
- Null product throws NullPointerException

(Implement ProductValidator too)
exercises.starter_code: // Implement ProductValidator and Product, then write 4 tests
hints: assertDoesNotThrow(() -> methodCall()) for the happy path
One test per validation rule
assertThrows returns the exception for message checking

exercises.title: Test a stock P&L calculator
exercises.instructions:
Write tests for `StockPosition.getPnL()` and `getPnLPercent()`:
- Buy 10 shares at ₹2760, current price ₹3890 → P&L=₹11300, %=40.9%
- Loss scenario: buy at ₹1600, current ₹1560 → P&L=-₹1200, %=-2.5%
- Zero position → P&L=0

Use delta for double comparisons.
hints: assertEquals(expected, actual, delta) for doubles

exercises.title: @BeforeAll for expensive setup
exercises.scenario: One-time expensive setup shared by all tests.
exercises.instructions:
Write tests for a `UserRepository` (in-memory, populated once). Use @BeforeAll to load 3 users, then test:
- findById returns correct user
- findById unknown id returns empty
- findAll returns 3 users
- count returns 3
hints: @BeforeAll must be static; field must be static too
Use this when setup is expensive and data is read-only across tests
Optional.orElseThrow() in test — fails with NoSuchElementException if empty

exercises.title: assertEquals argument order matters
exercises.scenario: Expected first, actual second — failure message depends on order.
exercises.expected_solution:
Wrong order: expected: <42> but was: <7>
Correct order: expected: <7> but was: <42>

test_cases.expected:
Wrong order: expected: <42> but was: <7>
Correct order: expected: <7> but was: <42>

hints: assertEquals(expected, actual) — expected is what you WANT, actual is what you GOT
Wrong order: 'expected 42 but was 7' — confusing! You expected 7, not 42
Correct order: 'expected 7 but was 42' — clear!

exercises.title: Test an order pricing engine
exercises.scenario: Complex business logic needs thorough testing.
exercises.instructions:
Write 5 tests for `OrderPricer`:
- Base price = qty × unit price
- Discount 10% if qty >= 10
- Discount 20% if qty >= 50
- Tax 18% added to discounted price
- Zero qty throws IllegalArgumentException

Test each rule independently.
hints: One test per business rule
Use delta 0.01 for double comparisons
Boundary test: exactly 10 (discount applies) and exactly 49 (10% not 20%)

exercises.title: assertNotNull and assertTrue for collections
exercises.scenario: Testing collection return values needs appropriate assertions.
exercises.instructions:
Write tests for `ProductSearch.search(String query)` returning `List<Product>`:
- Search 'mouse' returns non-null, non-empty list
- All results contain 'mouse' (case insensitive)
- Search '' returns empty list
- Search 'zzznotfound' returns empty list
hints: assertNotNull then assertFalse(isEmpty()) for non-empty list
forEach inside test can do per-element assertion
assertTrue with message for clear failure output

exercises.title: Complete BankAccount test suite
exercises.scenario: Thorough test coverage for a business-critical class.
exercises.instructions:
Write a full test suite (7+ tests) for BankAccount covering:
- Constructor sets initial balance
- Multiple deposits accumulate correctly
- Withdraw reduces balance
- Overdraft throws with exact message
- Negative deposit throws
- Close prevents further operations (3 assertions)
- Transfer between two accounts
hints: Transfer test needs TWO accounts — create second in test body
assertAll for closedAccount covers all three ops at once
Negative deposit uses check() which throws IllegalStateException

exercises.title: Test CSV parser edge cases
exercises.scenario: The parseCSVLine function needs exhaustive testing.
exercises.instructions:
Write 6 tests for `parseCSVLine` (implement or use the Topic 11.3 version):
- Simple unquoted line: 3 fields
- Quoted field with comma: still 2 fields
- Escaped quote inside quoted: single quote in result
- Empty field: empty string in result
- Quoted empty field: empty string in result
- Single field no comma: 1-element list
hints: Each test covers exactly one edge case
Escaped quote in Java string: \"\"\" = the characters: """ (two actual double quotes)
After escaped quote parse: 'Dell 27" Monitor' (one quote, not two)

exercises.title: Full order system test suite — Module 12.1 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Complete test coverage for an order processing system.
exercises.instructions:
Write a comprehensive test suite (8+ tests) for an `OrderService`:
- Place order: returns order with generated ID, status=PENDING
- Place order 0 qty: throws IllegalArgumentException
- Place order negative price: throws
- Get order by ID: returns correct order
- Get order unknown ID: throws IllegalArgumentException
- Cancel PENDING order: status becomes CANCELLED
- Cancel SHIPPED order: throws (cannot cancel shipped)
- Total orders count updates after placement

Implement OrderService to make all tests pass.
hints: OrderService needs to expose orders map for SHIPPED test — make it package-private
Each test is independent via @BeforeEach (fresh service)
Generated ID: assertNotNull(o.id()) — don't hardcode 'ORD-1'
